g.data-narration: Welcome to joining strings in Java. Learn efficient string manipulation with String.join and StringJoiner.
g.text: Efficient String Manipulation with String.join() and StringJoiner

g.data-narration: Java strings are immutable. Each concatenation with + creates new objects, causing performance issues in loops.
g.text: // Inefficient: Creates new object each iteration
result += i; // O(n²) time, O(n²) memory!

g.data-narration: Java provides two optimized solutions: String.join() for simple cases and StringJoiner for complex formatting.
g.text: Static utility for simple delimiter joining
Best for: CSV, JSON, SQL formatting

g.data-narration: String.join() has two overloaded forms: one for varargs and one for iterables like lists and sets.
Join array elements easily with String.join(). Specify delimiter and array - it handles the iteration internally.
String.join() works seamlessly with collections like List and Set, making it versatile for real-world scenarios.
StringJoiner offers two constructors - simple delimiter-only or advanced with custom prefix and suffix for formatting.
StringJoiner provides essential methods: add() appends elements, merge() combines joiners, and length() returns size.
StringJoiner's prefix and suffix enable JSON arrays, SQL IN clauses, and other formatted output with brackets.
Performance tests show StringBuilder, join(), and StringJoiner are 100-300x faster than + operator for 100k operations.
See practical code examples showing how to build CSV output and combine multiple collections efficiently.
StringJoiner is used for URL parameter building, JSON serialization, logging systems, and database query formatting.
Key points: prefer join methods over + operator, use StringJoiner for formatting, and remember null handling differences.
Remember: String.join() converts null to string 'null', while StringJoiner.add(null) throws NullPointerException.
You're now equipped to handle efficient string joining in Java. Practice with different scenarios to master these methods.
g.text: Use String.join() for simple cases • StringJoiner for complex formatting
